title: Our Course Project
text:
I’m honored to be a member of the NRG project team.
Below, you’ll find a brief summary of our project. To access a detailed project description.
Summary
Our project investigates the impact of electric vehicles (EVs) on reducing CO2 emissions and evaluates their integration within Turkey. EVs are widely acknowledged for their environmental benefits, particularly their potential to significantly reduce transportation-related carbon emissions. This study emphasizes that while the adoption of EVs can play a crucial role in emission reduction, its effectiveness is closely tied to factors such as population density and the transition to renewable energy in electricity generation. Please check our project website



 Back to top

title: About Me
text:
WHO IS MUSTAFA ALP ULAŞ?
I am a senior Industrial Engineering student at Hacettepe University with a strong focus on data-driven decision making, process improvement, and operational efficiency. Throughout my academic and professional journey, I have developed a solid foundation in optimization, business intelligence, and quality management, which I actively apply to real-world engineering and logistics problems.
I gained hands-on experience through my internships at ASELSAN and Mercedes-Benz Türk, where I worked on project quality management and logistics planning processes. During these experiences, I designed and implemented Power BI dashboards to monitor key performance indicators (KPIs), transforming raw operational data into meaningful insights. By automating reporting processes and improving data consistency, I contributed to more efficient and transparent decision-making environments.
At ASELSAN, I focused on quality management systems and review monitoring processes, developing interactive dashboards to track review cycles and participation metrics. This experience strengthened my understanding of quality assurance, traceability, and performance monitoring in complex engineering environments.
At Mercedes-Benz Türk, I worked on internal and external material flow planning, where I developed KPI dashboards integrating multiple logistics metrics. My work reduced manual reporting time significantly while ensuring full data consistency, enhancing the overall efficiency of the reporting process.
Currently, I am working on my graduation project in collaboration with UPS, where I am developing a mathematical optimization model to minimize transportation costs under vehicle capacity and service constraints. I am implementing this model using Python and Gurobi, and further improving it with metaheuristic approaches and real-world data integration. This project reflects my strong interest in operations research, logistics optimization, and decision support systems.
From a technical perspective, I have experience with Python (Pandas, NumPy), R, SQL, Power BI, and Excel VBA. I am particularly interested in combining analytical tools with engineering principles to solve complex operational problems and create scalable, data-driven solutions.
Beyond my professional interests, I actively engage in sports and competitive activities such as football, tennis, and table tennis, which strengthen my discipline, teamwork, and strategic thinking skills. I also enjoy downhill biking, which reflects my enthusiasm for challenges and dynamic environments, as well as playing FPS games that enhance my focus, quick decision-making, and reflexes.
I am a proactive and analytical thinker who values continuous improvement, structured problem solving, and effective communication. I enjoy working in dynamic environments where I can contribute to both operational excellence and strategic decision-making.
I am especially interested in opportunities related to project quality management, logistics and supply chain optimization, and business intelligence. My goal is to contribute to large-scale, data-driven operations, particularly in industries such as defense, manufacturing, and transportation, where efficiency, precision, and quality are critical.
I am always open to connecting with professionals, exchanging ideas, and exploring opportunities where I can create value through data, optimization, and engineering.

title: My Assignments

The most recent update to this page was made on October 22, 2024



 Back to top

title: Assignment 1
text:
(a)
I chose the video where Baykal Hafizoglu was the guest.
Summary
To begin with, Baykal Hafizoglu introduced himself. He talked about his academic achievements, work experiences and hobbies. He talked about the projects that took part in. He mentioned that he was both the owner and developer of a program called Supply Chain Solver. Secondly, he talked about data analytics world. He divided the analytics world in four pieces such as Descriptive, Diagnostic, Predictive, Prescriptive. He even compared Prescriptive Analytics to a doctor writing a prescription.He related the data analytics world pieces to the industrial engineering courses and emphasized the importance of these relationships. Thirdly, he talked about analytical solutions and methods. He gave some examples which is given at daily life about analytical methods. In addition, he talked about his obtainments. He recommended defining the problem first in all cases. He suggested that if I was developing a model, I should share that model with people. He said that staying on the computer would not be beneficial. He said that if I am doing a job, I have to present it in a language that everyone can understand. He said that after all the work, it would be useful to go back to problem definition. Finally, He answered questions from students such as Aykut.
First Question

Can you give an example of the Descriptive->Predictive->Prescriptive process from your daily life?

Answer: “Daily Inventory Level -> Demand Forecast -> Safety Stock Optimization” or “Mortgage Application -> Applicant’s Loan Score Estimation -> Mortgage Decision”
Second Question

What is KPI short for?



Key Personalizing Index
Kit Personal intensiveness
Key Performance Indicator (This is the answer)
Knot Pair Import

Third Step
Task 3: Calculate and display the total number of NA values in the entire data set. I used is.na function. I knew this function from lecture slides and Aykut’s Website. (By the way, Aykut’s Website is superfly)

total_number_of_none_available <- sum(is.na(polls_us_election_2016))
print(total_number_of_none_available)

[1] 11604


Fourth Step
Task 4: Replace all NA values with my first name and my birth year. First of all, I defined two variable which are my first name and my birth year. Secondly, I defined a function which is recursive function type. Mission of function is to evaluate of data variables equal to NA value or not for factor columns and this function has for loop with if-else statement for checking all columns and changing NA values. This function changed all NA values in numeric columns, character columns and factor columns with my birth year and my first name. Function returns the data in the end of loop. Finally, I assigned a variable which is equal to changed data by this function. In this part, I take some helps from slides, AI tools, datacamp lectures and friends about loop structure, function structure, use of if-else statement, use of return statement, use of squared brackets and use of levels function. Especially, I used AI on using for loop. I know for loops structure, but using if-else and functions inside each other in the for loop structure was very difficult for me. Recursive function idea was coming from my mind after 20 or 30 trials. Most of my error is state values changed by numeric value. I still don’t understand why I’m getting an error.

birth_year <- 2003
first_name <- "Mustafa"


replace_na_general <- function(data, birth_year, first_name) {
  
  
  replace_na_in_factor <- function(factor_col, replacement) {
    levels(factor_col) <- c(levels(factor_col), replacement) 
    factor_col[is.na(factor_col)] <- replacement             
    return(factor_col)
  }
  
  
  for (col in names(data)) {
    if (is.numeric(data[[col]])) {
      
      data[[col]][is.na(data[[col]])] <- birth_year
    } else if (is.character(data[[col]])) {
      
      data[[col]][is.na(data[[col]])] <- first_name
    } else if (is.factor(data[[col]])) {
      
      data[[col]] <- replace_na_in_factor(data[[col]], first_name)
    }
  }
  
  return(data)
}

modified_data <- replace_na_general(polls_us_election_2016, birth_year, first_name)

I checked in step five whether the code worked or not.
Fifth Step
Task 5: Print the first 10 rows of the new data frame and the total number of NAs remaining in the new data frame. In this step, I checked the code about working correctly. If the result is equal to 0, we have no NA values. They have all changed. I remember we did this kind of check in class. I used head function again for new data.

total_na_number_new <- sum(is.na(modified_data))
print(total_na_number_new)

[1] 0
